exemplar.title: How can different hull shapes be optimized to minimize material usage while ensuring sufficient buoyancy for a fishing boat?

detailedExemplarData.title: How can different hull shapes be optimized to minimize material usage while ensuring sufficient buoyancy for a fishing boat?

subject.title: Mathematics Analysis and Approaches (AA)
subject.description:
**IB Mathematics Analysis and Approaches** is a **Group 5** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **number and algebra, functions, geometry and trigonometry, statistics and probability, and calculus**, with a strong focus on **theoretical understanding, formal mathematical reasoning, and analytical problem-solving**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Non-calculator written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 2** | Calculator allowed written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 3** | Calculator allowed problem-solving | N/A | 60 minutes | 20% (HL only) |
| **Mathematical Exploration** | Internal Assessment | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: Research question is concise and provides a solid foundation.
Clear sequence of aim, assumptions, modelling sections and conclusion builds logical coherence.
Use of headings, numbered subsections and tables gives adequate structure.
Figures and tables are placed close to relevant text, aiding navigation.
weaknesses: Background theory is overly lengthy and occasionally repetitive, detracting from conciseness.
Candidate details (name, number, session) are missing from the title page.
Table of contents has formatting glitches and missing page numbers.
Units are sometimes omitted (e.g., thickness value), causing ambiguity.
Minor grammatical errors and misaligned table headers reduce professional polish.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All presentation strands were assessed at band 3: the exploration is coherent and well organized, with mostly relevant material. However, some background repetition, formatting inconsistencies and missing IBârequired details prevent it from reaching the highest level. A holistic score of 3/4 reflects these strengths and areas for improvement.

strengths: Variables, integrals and algebraic notation are used correctly in most instances.
Graphs from GeoGebra and tables of constants complement the algebraic work effectively.
Derivations are laid out step-by-step, allowing the reader to follow the logic.
Units are attached in place, demonstrating awareness of dimensional accuracy.
weaknesses: Inconsistent variable notation (e.g., âqâ vs. âgâ) leads to occasional confusion.
Axis labels and legends are missing from figures, reducing interpretability.
Unexplained rounding and omitted steps interrupt the logical flow.
Decimal commas are used instead of points, and unit notation is not always uniform.
Some integrals lack proper brackets, affecting mathematical precision.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Mathematical communication is consistently clear but has minor lapses in notation, representation and precision. All strands were placed in band 3; the work demonstrates good logical structure and mostly consistent notation. A holistic score of 3/4 accurately captures this level of performance.

strengths: The personal anecdote about childhood boat observations demonstrates genuine engagement.
Choosing and modelling three distinct hull forms shows significant independent thinking.
Use of GeoGebra to construct and analyse shapes reflects initiative and creativity.
weaknesses: The mathematics employed is standard and does not push beyond routine calculus.
There is limited discussion of alternative personal approaches or iterative refinement.
No advanced optimization or simulation tools were explored to deepen the personal approach.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Evidence of independent thinking, a clear personal narrative and creative modelling place the work solidly in band 2 for personal engagement. However, it does not reach the innovative or boundary-pushing level required for the highest band, so 2/3 is appropriate.

strengths: Meaningful reflection appears in the conclusion and limitations sections.
The student recognises modelling assumptions and their impact on results.
Clear evaluation of limitations with suggestions for FEA and empirical testing.
weaknesses: Critical analysis of results is brief and largely descriptive rather than probing.
No sensitivity study or deeper interrogation of error sources is performed.
Reflection lacks the depth and insight required for substantial critical evaluation.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Depth of reflection and evaluation of outcomes are at band 2, but critical analysis is limited (band 1). On balance, the work demonstrates meaningful reflection without the depth for band 3, warranting a score of 2/3.

strengths: The use of geometry, integrals and polynomial solutions is appropriate and effectively supports the investigation.
Mathematical techniques are commensurate with SL level and applied correctly in most cases.
Calculations demonstrate good understanding, with sound methodology throughout.
weaknesses: Minor errors in labeling constants and occasional rounding mistakes introduce small inaccuracies.
Key algebraic steps (e.g., root justification, expansion details) are omitted, reducing rigor.
No higher-level HL content is attempted, and some derivations assume unexplained shortcuts.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Relevance of mathematics is strong (band 4), but level appropriateness and understanding contain minor lapses (band 3 each). The holistic quality of the mathematics aligns with band 3 (âgood understanding with minor errorsâ), so a score of 3/6 accurately reflects the work.

strandSelections.reasoning: Within each section, derivations are laid out step-by-step and integrals are evaluated logically, allowing the reader to follow the argument. Occasional algebraic shortcuts and unexplained rounding prevent the communication from being âexcellentâ, but clarity is generally good.

Calculations are generally correct and demonstrate sound understanding, yet there are a few inconsistencies (e.g., mis-labelled constants, minor rounding mistakes). These minor errors place the strand at âgood understanding with minor errorsâ rather than fully correct.

The student employs graphs from GeoGebra, tables of constants and algebraic/functional forms for each hull. These representations complement each other effectively, though no 3-D diagrams or spreadsheet output is provided, so the use is effective but not âhighly effectiveâ.

The exploration follows a clear sequence: aim, assumptions, three modelling sections, conclusion and limitations. Arguments generally build on one another and the reader can follow how each hull is treated. Minor logical gaps (e.g. sudden declaration that three polynomial roots exist without discussing the two rejected values) prevent it from reaching the exemplary standard, but overall coherence is solid.

Modelling three hull forms, integrating areas, and comparing results display creativity and initiative above a routine treatment, yet no advanced optimisation techniques or simulation tools are attempted, capping the strand at a good rather than exceptional level.

Analysis of why results differ (e.g., discussing drag or pressure distributions) is brief and largely descriptive; the student does not interrogate mathematical errors or sensitivity. Hence only basic critical analysis is demonstrated.

The student identifies uniform-thickness and shape-range limitations and suggests FEA and empirical testingâconstituting a clear evaluation with implications for further work. The evaluation is appropriate but not exhaustive, situating it in the competent band.

Reflection appears in the conclusion and limitations sections, where the student comments on surprising findings and recognises modelling assumptions. This reflection is meaningful and threaded through several parts, though not sufficiently probing to be termed âsubstantialâ.

Variables, integrals and algebraic expressions are usually written correctly, and units are attached in several places. A few slips (âqâ instead of âgâ, missing absolute-value notation in one integral) show that usage is âmostly consistentâ rather than perfect.

The narrative links the studentâs childhood fascination to methodological choices, indicating a clear personal approach. However, there is limited reflection on alternative personal decisions or iterative refinement, so the engagement is good but not âhighly innovativeâ.

Most material directly supports the investigation, but some descriptive history of hulls and repeated explanations of Archimedesâ principle add length without new insight. Thus the work is largely relevant yet not consistently concise.

Geometry, integrals and polynomial solutions are all appropriate to the problem and are applied correctly in most cases, showing effective use of relevant mathematics. However, no higher-level HL content such as differential equations or multivariable optimisation appears, so the work is effective rather than comprehensive or expert.

Choosing a personally motivated question and devising original models for three hulls demonstrate noticeable independent thinking, yet the mathematics remains standard and does not push boundaries; therefore evidence is significant but not outstanding.

Headings, numbered subsections and a table of variables provide a recognisable structure; figures and tables appear close to the relevant text. Nevertheless, inconsistent page labelling and occasional formatting glitches in the ToC mean the structure is good rather than exceptional.

The techniques (basic calculus, area/volume formulae) are standard for the HL course but do not exploit the more demanding aspects of HL content. Accordingly the mathematics is commensurate with course level, but not fully or expertly so.

annotations.comment: The title is clear but lacks candidate details like student name, candidate number, and examination session. Including these is essential for IB IA presentation requirements.
Figure 1 effectively illustrates hull diversity, but lacks axis labels and a legend identifying each shape. Annotating axes and hull types would enhance interpretability.
The research question is concise, focused and clearly defines the aim of the investigation, providing a solid foundation for the exploration.
The table of contents is present but page numbers are missing beside entries and columns are misaligned. Adding clear page references and uniform formatting will improve navigation.
The personal anecdote describing childhood observations of boats adds authentic engagement and motivates the exploration from a genuine perspective.
The equation $$F_{buoyancy}=F_{weight}$$ is clear, but defining each forceâs units explicitly would enhance precision and prevent misinterpretation.
There is a typo: âCanceling $$q$$â should refer to gravity $$g$$. Consistent variable notation is crucial to avoid confusion in derivations.
This paragraph restates standard buoyancy concepts at length. Consider condensing background theory to focus more quickly on the unique aspects of your modelling approach.
The aim statement on this page repeats content from the previous section. Merging or rephrasing overlapping aims would improve conciseness and flow.
The expansion to $$V_{	ext{inner}}=4T^3+21.8T^2+30.8T+12.25$$ is abrupt. Including key algebraic steps strengthens the rigor of your derivation.
Figure 2 shows the polynomial graph but lacks axis labels and scale markers. Adding these will help readers interpret the functionâs behavior correctly.
After calculating $$V_b=4.5371	ext{ m}^3$$, stating the unit explicitly would remove any ambiguity about the volume measure.
Mentioning GeoGebra demonstrates initiative, but citing the version and specific settings used would enhance methodological transparency.
The domain is written as â2.5 â¥ x â¥ 0â. Standard mathematical convention orders inequalities as â0 â¤ x â¤ 2.5â for improved clarity.
After presenting the integral, include the evaluated numerical result for the cross-sectional area here to help the reader follow the calculation.
Units such as âkgm^-3â should be formatted consistently as $$000	ext{ kg m}^{-3}$$. Uniform unit notation enhances mathematical communication.
Table 1 is well structured but the columns and header styles are misaligned. Standardizing cell widths and formatting will improve readability.
You note three polynomial roots but do not explain why two are rejected. Explicitly justifying the selection of the physically reasonable root would clarify your reasoning.
In simplifying the quadratic in Equation (11), constants and factors of Ï are condensed. Showing one extra algebraic step would clarify how coefficients arise.
Decimal commas (â1,25â) are used in limits and expressions. Standardizing to decimal points (â1.25â) improves international readability.
Decimal commas appear again in Equation (2) notation. Converting all to decimal points will ensure consistent mathematical style.
The conclusion effectively summarizes results but lacks a comparative summary of thickness values. Adding a table of final thicknesses would strengthen the comparative analysis.
Expressing $$	frac{49}{6}$$ as a decimal (approximately 8.167âmÂ³) alongside the fraction would make the displaced volume more immediately accessible.
When substituting into Equation (1), explicitly write out $$1000	imes rac{49}{6}=m_{	ext{boat}}+600$$ to clearly show each step.
The solution $$T=0.16014$$ is clear, but indicate units (m or cm) directly with the value to avoid any ambiguity.
There is a grammatical error: âmust could beâ should read âmust beâ. Refining such phrasing will improve the professional tone.
Parameters $a,b,c,d$ are used inconsistently for ellipse center and axes. Adopting distinct notation (e.g. $(h,k)$ for center) would clarify your equations.
The integral notation in Equation (4) is missing a closing bracket around the integrand. Properly bracketed expressions ensure mathematical accuracy.
The caption for Figure 4 appears in an equation block. Separating caption text from the mathematical environment will prevent confusion.
You reflect on uniform thickness as a limitation, but deeper critical analysisâsuch as a sensitivity study on thickness variationâwould enhance the evaluation section.
The bibliography cites only one source. Incorporating additional peer-reviewed references on hull design and mathematical modelling will strengthen credibility.
